content.full_text:
KCBS

CBS Radio
A Division of CBS Inc.
One Embarcadero Center
San Francisco, California 94111
(415) 982-7000

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

Enclosed are copies of your recent reply to an
earlier KCBS editorial. We are providing copies
of both statements for your convenience.

We appreciate your interest and attention. KCBS
believes an exchange of views in an atmosphere of
mutual respect is a healthy assurance the public
will remain informed and aware of significant issues.

Cordially,

Sydney Nickerson
Director
Editorial & Public Affairs

Mr. Daniel Lowenstein
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

July 1, 1976


KCBS NEWSRADIO 74 [logo] ONE EMBARCADERO CENTER
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94111
PHONE: (415) 362-7000 CBS OWNED

KCBS Radio welcomes editorial
replies by qualified spokespersons
for the purpose of further stimulating
public discussion and maintaining
fairness and balance in the
presentation of public issues.
If you missed the broadcast of this
reply to a KCBS Radio editorial,
we hope you will read it.
Your comments are always
most welcome.

Peter M. McCoy, Vice President
CBS Radio Division
General Manager, KCBS Radio

EDITORIAL
REPLY

SUBJECT: Voters Pamphlet
1R 76-49

SPEAKER: Daniel Lowenstein

BROADCAST: June 24, 1976
12:18 am, 2:46 am,
5:18 am, 8:18 am, 12:18 pm, 3:18 pm, 5:18 pm, and 9:18 pm

ON JUNE 18, 1976, KCBS DISCUSSED THE POTENTIAL FOR INACCURACY
IN THE ARGUMENTS PRESENTED IN THE CALIFORNIA VOTERS PAMPHLET.
SPEAKING IN RESPONSE IS DANIEL LOWENSTEIN, CHAIRMAN OF THE FAIR
POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION.

Every voter can sympathize with the KCBS criticism of some of
the pro and con arguments in the state voters' handbook.
Nevertheless, long experience has shown that the medicine
proposed by KCBS -- censorship by the Fair Political Practices
Commission or the Secretary of State -- would be much worse
than the disease.

The Political Reform Act of 1974, better known as Proposition
Nine, which contains campaign disclosures, conflict of interest
and lobbyist regulation provisions, also brought about major
improvements in the voters' handbook. There is now at least
an attempt to write the summaries in ordinary English rather
than legalese.

The pro and con arguments are an important supplement to
the official summaries. Sure the arguments are sometimes
inaccurate or exaggerated, but each side is given an opportunity
for rebuttal. In extreme cases the courts may correct inaccuracies.
On the other hand, if an administrative agency were given the
power to censor the arguments, there would be constant
controversy and a danger of serious abuse. The same would be
the case if a single, supposedly neutral group prepared pro
and con arguments as KCBS also suggested. It is better to
let both sides have their say and then let the voters decide.
If that is what KCBS calls "passing the buck," then so be it.


KCBS NEWSRADIO 74

ONE EMBARCADERO CENTER
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94111
PHONE: (415) 982-7000 CBS OWNED

EDITORIAL

This is one of a continuing series of KCBS Radio editorials expressing
the opinion of station management on topics of vital interest to the
community. Responsible representatives of opposing viewpoints are given
the opportunity to reply on the air. If you missed the broadcast of this
editorial, we hope you will read it. Your comments are always welcome.

Peter M. McCoy, Vice President, CBS Radio Division
General Manager, KCBS Radio

SUBJECT:          Voter Handbook
                  #76-49

BROADCAST:        June 18, 1976

                  12:18am, 2:46am, 5:18am, 8:18am, 12:18pm, 3:18pm, 5:18pm,
                  and 9:18pm


                  During the last election how many voters do you suppose
                  noticed the sentence in the state voters' handbook which
                  read "Arguments printed on this page are the opinions of
                  the authors and have not been checked for accuracy by any
                  official agency".

                  Some of those authors the book referred to, were legislators
                  running for re-election.  Others were individuals or groups
                  with a special interest in the arguments.  That doesn't lend
                  itself to unbiased or necessarily accurate statements.
                  KCBS thinks voters are being sold short on ballot information.

                  The voters pamphlet is prepared by the Secretary of State.
                  Though she is required to print both a pro and con argument,
                  Mrs. Eu is responsible for copy only, not content.  Though
                  anyone may submit a statement for the handbook, the legislator
                  who authored the ballot measure usually writes the argument.
                  Special interest groups also get priority.  There's a 20 day
                  period during which anyone may rebut these statements.

                  Even with that, both the Secretary of State and the Fair
                  Political Practices Commission say they can't take responsibility
                  for the contents.  If we're going to spend money to print a
                  statewide voters guide it should be both accurate and objective.

                  KCBS thinks this responsibility should be given to a third
                  party.  We suggest the League of Women Voters.  The League has
                  a long-standing reputation for giving fair arguments on both
                  sides of political issues.  We think they would handle the
                  task well, without passing the buck.  And that would serve us
                  better than the Secretary of State's office or the Fair Political
                  Practices Commission has.
